content.text: Why Capacity Review Needs a Formal Cadence

Capacity for GenAI services is not self-managing. GPU provisioning requires a 14-day lead time; demand forecasts carry significant uncertainty at 90 days; and token throughput — the binding resource — changes in step-function increments when product teams ship new features. Without a structured review cadence, capacity decisions happen reactively — either after a saturation incident or during the stress of a launch, when there is no time to procure hardware.
A structured cadence has three purposes:
Ensures provisioning decisions happen ahead of the GPU lead time (14 days minimum, 30 days preferred for reserved instances).
Creates a documented audit trail of capacity decisions that SRE, engineering, and finance teams can reference during budget reviews.
Closes the loop between product roadmap changes (feature launches, context window expansions) and infrastructure planning.

attrs.alt: Capacity review cadence: weekly runway check, monthly 90-day forecast, quarterly hardware budget, ad-hoc triggered reviews

content.text: The Four-Cadence Review Structure
Weekly Review (Every Monday)

The weekly review is the primary operational check. It takes 15–30 minutes and is run by the SRE on-call engineer before the team standup. The goal is to catch any model whose runway has dropped below 30 days since last week and open a Jira provisioning ticket before the matter becomes urgent.
Agenda Item
Time
Output
Runway check per model (automated Slack report)
5 min
List of models by runway
GPU utilization trend (7-day chart)
5 min
Flag any >75% MFU trend
Token budget deltas since last week
5 min
Flag new features in rollout
Open/update Jira tickets if runway < 30d
10 min
Ticket INFRA-XXXX created
Trigger rule:
If any model's runway drops below 30 days and no Jira ticket exists, the on-call SRE opens one before the standup ends. This is non-negotiable.
Monthly Review (First Friday)

The monthly review is a 60-minute working session between the SRE lead and engineering manager. It uses a 90-day Prophet forecast (P80 upper bound) to identify whether current capacity will be sufficient at the end of the next quarter, and whether any reserved instance commitments need to be modified.
90-day forecast per model — compare against current capacity with a 30% headroom target
Reserved instance review — are we over- or under-committed on 1-year reservations?
Cost efficiency audit — $ per million output tokens per model (flag if > 2× cheapest equivalent)
Roadmap feature impact estimates — collect token budget estimates from PM for any feature shipping in the next 60 days
If 90d headroom plan shows shortfall: escalate to quarterly process and open hardware budget request
Quarterly Review (Week 1 of Quarter)

The quarterly review is a 90-minute session with the SRE lead, engineering director, and (optionally) a finance partner. It drives the hardware budget request for the next two quarters and decides whether the GPU pool needs rebalancing between model families.
180-day forecast with P80 upper bound — basis for hardware budget
GPU pool rebalancing — should large-model capacity be converted to small-model capacity (or vice versa) as the traffic mix shifts?
Model right-sizing audit — are there requests that could be served by a 7B model instead of a 70B model, reducing GPU cost by 4–5×?
Reserved instance commitment decisions — lock in 1-year reservations for stable baseline; leave burst headroom on spot
Hardware budget document produced: GPU count, model, timeline, cost justification
Ad-Hoc Triggered Reviews
Four events trigger an unscheduled capacity review regardless of cadence:
Runway alert fires
(< 14 days for any model) — same-day review, page EM if not in business hours
Feature launch planned
within 30 days — require token budget estimate from PM before approving launch; pre-scale capacity 24h before ship
Major traffic incident
where root cause was capacity saturation — post-mortem includes capacity section with forward-looking plan
Model upgrade
that changes GPU memory footprint (e.g., 7B → 70B, or adding a new model to the fleet) — re-run headroom calculation before deploying to production
Headroom Targets and Calculation
Headroom is the fraction of capacity not consumed by current demand. It exists to absorb burst traffic, provide scale-up time, and buffer forecast uncertainty. The targets differ by time horizon:
Time Horizon
Target Utilization
Headroom
Reason
Operational (today)
Absorb burst; HPA lag is 2-4 min
30-day plan
70% of P80 forecast
Forecast ±20%; provision to upper bound
90-day plan
70% of P80 forecast
Forecast ±35%; upper bound is conservative
Feature launch
60% post-launch
Launch bursts 1.5-3× normal; unknown distribution

At 80% GPU MFU and above, P95 latency degrades non-linearly because batching efficiency falls and requests queue. Provisioning to 70% utilization targets keeps latency in the SLO range even during moderate burst demand.

# Auto-create Jira provisioning ticket when runway drops below threshold
import os, json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import urllib.request, urllib.parse

JIRA_BASE = os.environ['JIRA_BASE_URL']          # e.g. https://myorg.atlassian.net
JIRA_TOKEN = os.environ['JIRA_API_TOKEN']
JIRA_EMAIL = os.environ['JIRA_EMAIL']
JIRA_PROJECT = 'INFRA'

def create_provisioning_ticket(
    model: str,
    runway_days: float,
    gpus_needed: int,
    shortfall_toks_per_s: float,
) -> str:
    due = (datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(days=max(1, runway_days - 14))).strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
    body = {
        'fields': {
            'project': {'key': JIRA_PROJECT},
            'summary': f'[GenAI Capacity] {model} runway {runway_days:.0f}d -- request {gpus_needed} A100s',
            'description': {
                'type': 'doc', 'version': 1,
                'content': [{'type': 'paragraph', 'content': [{
                    'type': 'text',
                    'text': (
                        f'Model {model} has {runway_days:.0f} days until capacity saturation. '
                        f'Shortfall at 90d forecast: {shortfall_toks_per_s:.0f} tok/s. '
                        f'Requesting {gpus_needed} additional A100 80GB nodes. '
                        f'Required by: {due} (14d lead time before runway exhaustion).'
                    )
                }]}]
            },
            'issuetype': {'name': 'Task'},
            'priority': {'name': 'High' if runway_days < 21 else 'Medium'},
            'duedate': due,
            'labels': ['genai-capacity', 'gpu-provisioning'],
        }
    }
    creds = urllib.parse.quote(f'{JIRA_EMAIL}:{JIRA_TOKEN}')
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        f'{JIRA_BASE}/rest/api/3/issue',
        data=json.dumps(body).encode(),
        headers={
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'Authorization': f'Basic {creds}',
        },
        method='POST',
    )
    resp = json.loads(urllib.request.urlopen(req).read())
    return resp['key']  # e.g. 'INFRA-4291'
Cost-Headroom Tradeoff

Headroom is not free. A GPU running at 60% utilization has 40% idle capacity that costs money. The quarterly review evaluates whether the headroom target is calibrated correctly by comparing the cost of idle GPU capacity against the cost of saturation incidents:
An A100 80GB on a 1-year reservation costs approximately $1.80/hr (~$1,300/month).
30% headroom on a 4-GPU fleet is 1.2 GPU-equivalents of idle capacity: ~$390/month.
A P1 incident caused by capacity saturation typically costs 4–8 hours of SRE and engineering time plus potential customer SLA credits — well above $390.
The economically rational headroom target is higher than most teams instinctively choose. The quarterly review is the forum to defend this cost to finance.
Right-sizing opportunity:

The quarterly cost report frequently reveals that a subset of requests that are being served by the large model (70B) could be routed to a distilled model (7B) with no user-visible quality difference. A 7B model serves 4–5× more tokens/s per GPU. Routing even 30% of traffic to the small model can reduce fleet size by 1–2 GPUs without adding headroom risk.
Review Artifacts and Documentation

Each review cadence produces a documented output that is stored in a shared drive alongside the Jira ticket. The monthly and quarterly reviews produce a capacity plan document with a standard structure:
Current state:
utilization and runway per model, open tickets
Forecast:
30d and 90d token throughput with P50/P80 bands
Shortfall analysis:
models where P80 forecast exceeds 70% of current capacity
Recommended action:
GPU count, timeline, reserved vs. spot decision
Cost justification:
$ per million tokens before and after; idle cost vs. incident cost
Approval:
SRE lead sign-off for < $10k/month change; Director for > $10k/month
The weekly review does not produce a formal document — the Slack report and any Jira tickets opened are the record.
Summary

A capacity review cadence for GenAI services runs at four levels: weekly (15-min runway check with automated Slack report), monthly (60-min 90-day forecast review with reserved-instance and cost audit), quarterly (90-min 180-day hardware budget planning), and ad-hoc (triggered by runway alerts, feature launches, or incidents). The headroom target of 30% at 70% utilization is calibrated against GPU burst behavior at high MFU, HPA reaction time, and forecast uncertainty. Provisioning decisions are always based on the P80 forecast upper bound. Jira tickets are opened automatically when runway drops below 30 days, giving 16 days of buffer before the 14-day GPU lead time is breached.
